Death Swallowed Up of Life. Meaning of This Phrase in Relation to the Resurrection of the Body.

ccel.title: ANF03. Latin Christianity: Its Founder, Tertullian

source.title: ANF03. Latin Christianity: Its Founder, Tertullian

title: Death Swallowed Up of Life. Meaning of This Phrase in Relation to the Resurrection of the Body.

source.title: ANF03. Latin Christianity: Its Founder, Tertullian

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: Then, again, questions very often are suggested by occasional and isolated terms, just as much as they are by connected sentences.

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: Thus, because of the apostle’s expression, “that mortality may be swallowed up of life”—in reference to the flesh—they wrest the word swallowed up into the sense of the actual destruction of the flesh; as if we might not speak of ourselves as swallowing bile, or swallowing grief, meaning that we conceal and hide it, and keep it within ourselves.

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: The truth is, when it is written, “This mortal must put on immortality,” it is explained in what sense it is that “mortality is swallowed up of life”—even whilst, clothed with immortality, it is hidden and concealed, and contained within it, not as consumed, and destroyed, and lost.

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: But death, you will say in reply to me, at this rate, must be safe, even when it has been swallowed up.

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: Well, then, I ask you to distinguish words which are similar in form according to their proper meanings.

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: Death is one thing, and mortality is another.

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: It is one thing for death to be swallowed up, and another thing for mortality to be swallowed up.

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: Death is incapable of immortality, but not so mortality.

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: Besides, as it is written that “this mortal must put on immortality,” how is this possible when it is swallowed up of life?

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: But how is it swallowed up of life, (in the sense of destroyed by it) when it is actually received, and restored, and included in it?

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: For the rest, it is only just and right that death should be swallowed up in utter destruction, since it does itself devour with this same intent.

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: Death, says the apostle, has devoured by exercising its strength, and therefore has been itself devoured in the struggle “swallowed up in victory.”

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: O death, where is thy victory?”

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: Therefore life, too, as the great antagonist of death, will in the struggle swallow up for salvation what death, in its struggle, had swallowed up for destruction.
